Bovine Respiratory Disease (BRD).description: A complex of infections affecting the lungs, most common in stressed or recently transported cattle.
Bovine Respiratory Disease (BRD).treatment: Broad-spectrum antibiotics (florfenicol, tulathromycin, enrofloxacin). NSAIDs for inflammation. Isolation and supportive care.
Bovine Respiratory Disease (BRD).prevention: Vaccination (BRSV, IBR, PI3, BVDV), minimize stress during transport, proper ventilation.

Foot and Mouth Disease.description: Highly contagious viral disease causing blisters on feet, mouth, and teats. Notifiable disease.
Foot and Mouth Disease.treatment: No specific antiviral. Supportive care, wound cleaning, antibiotics to prevent secondary infection. NOTIFY AUTHORITIES IMMEDIATELY.
Foot and Mouth Disease.urgency: IMMEDIATE - Report to animal health authorities

Bovine Viral Diarrhea (BVD).description: Viral disease causing immune suppression, diarrhea, reproductive failure, and persistent infection in calves.
Bovine Viral Diarrhea (BVD).treatment: No specific treatment. Supportive therapy (fluids, electrolytes). Remove persistently infected (PI) animals.

Mastitis.description: Inflammation of the mammary gland, usually bacterial. Major cause of reduced milk yield.
Mastitis.treatment: Intramammary antibiotics, systemic antibiotics for severe cases. Teat stripping. NSAIDs for pain.
Mastitis.prevention: Post-milking teat dipping, dry cow therapy, clean environment, regular milking equipment maintenance.

Pneumonia.description: Lung infection, often caused by bacteria (Mannheimia, Pasteurella) or viruses.
Pneumonia.treatment: Antibiotics (florfenicol, oxytetracycline), NSAIDs, bronchodilators if needed.
Pneumonia.prevention: Reduce stress, good ventilation, vaccination, avoid overcrowding.

Bloat.description: Abnormal accumulation of gas in the rumen, can be frothy or free gas. Life-threatening if severe.
Bloat.treatment: Pass stomach tube to release gas. Anti-foaming agents (poloxalene, simethicone). Emergency trocarization if severe.
Bloat.prevention: Gradual introduction to lush pastures, use of anti-foaming agents, avoid wet legumes.

Blackleg.description: Fatal clostridial infection causing gas gangrene in muscles, primarily in young cattle.
Blackleg.treatment: Large doses of penicillin early. Prognosis usually poor once clinical signs appear.
Blackleg.urgency: IMMEDIATE - Usually fatal without very early treatment

Lumpy Skin Disease.description: Viral disease causing skin nodules, fever, and reduced production. Spread by insects.
Lumpy Skin Disease.treatment: No specific antiviral. Supportive care, antibiotics for secondary infections, wound care.

Anthrax.description: Bacterial disease (B. anthracis) causing sudden death. NOTIFIABLE DISEASE - extremely dangerous.
Anthrax.treatment: High-dose penicillin if caught early. Do NOT perform post-mortem - spores are hazardous to humans.

Brucellosis.description: Bacterial disease causing abortions, retained placenta, and reproductive failure. Zoonotic.
Brucellosis.treatment: Cull infected animals. No practical treatment for cattle.
Brucellosis.prevention: Vaccination (S19 or RB51 for heifers), test and slaughter policy.

Ringworm.description: Fungal skin infection causing circular lesions, common in young cattle in winter.
Ringworm.treatment: Topical antifungals (enilconazole, natamycin). Iodine solutions. Often self-limiting.
Ringworm.prevention: Good hygiene, avoid overcrowding, disinfect housing.

Pink Eye (IBK).description: Bacterial (Moraxella bovis) eye infection causing corneal ulcers and temporary blindness.
Pink Eye (IBK).treatment: Penicillin (subconjunctival injection or systemic), eye patching, fly control.
Pink Eye (IBK).prevention: Face fly control, vaccination, reduce dust and UV exposure.
Pink Eye (IBK).urgency: Treat within 48 hours to prevent blindness

Coccidiosis.description: Parasitic intestinal disease causing bloody diarrhea in calves, especially in overcrowded conditions.
Coccidiosis.treatment: Amprolium, sulfonamides, toltrazuril. Fluid therapy for dehydration.
Coccidiosis.prevention: Clean environment, avoid overcrowding, prophylactic treatment in high-risk groups.

Ketosis.description: Metabolic disease in early lactation cows due to negative energy balance, fat mobilization.
Ketosis.treatment: IV glucose, propylene glycol (oral), corticosteroids in severe cases. Address underlying nutrition.
Ketosis.prevention: Optimize body condition at calving, high-quality transition diet, monitor post-calving.

Milk Fever (Hypocalcemia).description: Metabolic disease at calving due to low blood calcium levels. Can progress to recumbency and death.
Milk Fever (Hypocalcemia).treatment: IV calcium borogluconate (slow infusion). Oral calcium supplements.
Milk Fever (Hypocalcemia).prevention: DCAD diet in dry period, calcium supplementation at calving, vitamin D.
Milk Fever (Hypocalcemia).urgency: IMMEDIATE - Can be fatal within hours

Leptospirosis.description: Bacterial disease causing fever, anemia, abortion, and kidney/liver failure. Zoonotic.
Leptospirosis.prevention: Vaccination, rodent control, avoid stagnant water.

IBR (Bovine Herpesvirus).description: Viral disease causing respiratory disease, conjunctivitis, and reproductive failure (IBR/IPV).
IBR (Bovine Herpesvirus).treatment: No specific antiviral. Supportive care, antibiotics for secondary infections.
IBR (Bovine Herpesvirus).prevention: Vaccination (MLV or killed), biosecurity, semen testing for breeding bulls.

Clostridial Diseases.description: Group of serious diseases (enterotoxemia, pulpy kidney, tetanus) caused by Clostridium spp.
Clostridial Diseases.treatment: Antitoxin if available. Penicillin. Usually poor prognosis once severe.

Tick Fever (Anaplasmosis).description: Tick-transmitted disease destroying red blood cells, causing severe anemia.
Tick Fever (Anaplasmosis).treatment: Oxytetracycline (long-acting), imidocarb diproprionate. Blood transfusion in severe cases.
Tick Fever (Anaplasmosis).prevention: Tick control (acaricides), strategic treatment, breed selection for resistance.

Pinkeye (Moraxella).description: Contagious eye infection causing watering, squinting, and clouding of the cornea.
Pinkeye (Moraxella).treatment: Penicillin injection (subconjunctival or IM), tetracycline eye ointment, eye patch.
Pinkeye (Moraxella).prevention: Face fly control, vaccination, minimize UV exposure.

Bovine Leukemia.description: Viral cancer of lymphoid tissue. Most infected cattle remain subclinical.
Bovine Leukemia.treatment: No treatment available. Cull affected animals.
Bovine Leukemia.prevention: Test and segregate/cull infected animals. Use clean needles and equipment.

Not Pregnant.description: The cow does not show signs consistent with pregnancy.
Not Pregnant.recommendations: Monitor estrus cycle (18-24 days). If breeding was attempted, confirm with PAG blood/milk test at 28+ days post-breeding. Consider reproductive examination if repeated failures.
Not Pregnant.care: Maintain normal nutrition and health management.
Not Pregnant.confidence_note: Behavioral observation alone is not definitive. Laboratory testing recommended.

Early Pregnancy (1-4 weeks).description: Signs consistent with early pregnancy (weeks 1-4 post-conception). Embryo is developing; heartbeat detectable by day 20-22.
Early Pregnancy (1-4 weeks).recommendations: Confirm with PAG blood/milk test (available from day 28) or ultrasound (from day 28-30). Minimize stress - embryonic loss risk is highest in first trimester.
Early Pregnancy (1-4 weeks).care: Maintain forage quality. Avoid drastic dietary changes. No rough handling or transport. Supplement with selenium and vitamin E.
Early Pregnancy (1-4 weeks).confidence_note: Signs are very subtle at this stage. Professional confirmation strongly advised.

Mid Pregnancy (4-8 weeks).description: Signs consistent with early-to-mid pregnancy. Major organs forming; placenta establishing nutrient transfer.
Mid Pregnancy (4-8 weeks).recommendations: Schedule ultrasound or PAG test if not done. Confirm fetal viability. Record expected calving date (~283 days from breeding).
Mid Pregnancy (4-8 weeks).care: Balanced nutrition with quality forage. Supplement minerals (selenium, copper, phosphorus, zinc). Vaccinate against BVD, IBR, leptospirosis if not current.
Mid Pregnancy (4-8 weeks).confidence_note: PAG blood test will give reliable confirmation at this stage.

Second Trimester (3-6 months).description: Mid-pregnancy confirmed. Fetus growing rapidly - may reach size of a small dog by month 6. Fetal movements may be felt on right flank.
Second Trimester (3-6 months).recommendations: Rectal palpation or ultrasound can confirm and assess fetal health. Monitor body condition score (BCS 2.5-3.5). Deworm if needed.
Second Trimester (3-6 months).care: Increase protein and energy slightly. Ensure adequate minerals. Separate from aggressive herd mates. Vaccinate 4-6 weeks before calving.
Second Trimester (3-6 months).confidence_note: Physical signs more reliable at this stage. Ultrasound confirms fetal sex after day 55.

Third Trimester (7-9 months).description: Advanced pregnancy. Fetus gaining >75% of birth weight during this period. Significant nutritional demands on the cow.
Third Trimester (7-9 months).recommendations: Prepare calving pen (clean, dry, well-bedded). Monitor closely. Check for signs of milk fever risk (especially dairy breeds). Pre-calving vaccination.
Third Trimester (7-9 months).care: Increase energy intake with quality forage and grains. Provide DCAD diet if high-risk for milk fever. Ensure adequate shelter. Daily observation.
Third Trimester (7-9 months).confidence_note: Signs are clear and reliable at this stage.

Imminent Calving.description: Calving expected within 24-72 hours. Fetus moving into birth position.
Imminent Calving.recommendations: Move to calving pen. Ensure colostrum management plan ready. Have calving equipment available. Contact vet if no progress after 2 hours of active labor.
Imminent Calving.care: 24/7 observation. Clean and disinfect calving area. Ensure cow is comfortable. Have calcium supplement ready for at-risk cows.
Imminent Calving.confidence_note: Signs are highly reliable at this stage.
